description: This line chart (Fig. 4) plots the evolution of average Schmid factors as a function of tensile engineering strain for an Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel at both room temperature (RT) and liquid nitrogen temperature (LNT). Four distinct curves are shown: RT {111}<110>γ (grey squares), RT {111}<112>γ (red circles), LNT {111}<110>γ (blue triangles), and LNT {111}<112>γ (green inverted triangles). At RT, the {111}<110>γ Schmid factor drops abruptly from approximately 0.438 to 0.424 by 3% strain and then remains nearly constant, while the {111}<112>γ factor decreases moderately and stabilizes near 0.434. In contrast, at LNT both {111}<110>γ and {111}<112>γ Schmid factors peak at roughly 10% strain (around 0.444 and 0.450, respectively) before declining continuously with further deformation, with the {111}<110>γ factor falling to about 0.410 at 55% strain. These divergent trends reveal a strong temperature and orientation dependence of resolved shear stress on active slip and twinning systems, providing quantitative evidence for the distinct deformation twinning mechanism and enhanced strain hardening observed under cryogenic conditions.
